title: Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992
jurisdiction: Great Britain (separate regulations for Northern Ireland)
chapters: SI 1992/3004. 27 Regulations + 1 Schedule in 6 Parts. Made under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (HSWA) ss.15, 80. Implements EU Workplace Directive 89/654/EEC. Supported by HSE Approved Code of Practice L24 (4th edition, 2013). The Regulations cover the PHYSICAL WORKING ENVIRONMENT — the building, its facilities, and the conditions within it. They apply to virtually every workplace in Great Britain: offices, factories, warehouses, shops, hospitals, schools, hotels, restaurants, and any other premises used as a place of work.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Statutory Instrument — made under HSWA 1974 ss.15, 80
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Made by the Secretary of State for Employment under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Originally came into force 1 January 1993 for NEW workplaces and modifications/extensions to existing workplaces. Extended to ALL existing workplaces from 1 January 1996. Since 31 December 1995, every workplace in Great Britain (with limited exceptions) has been required to comply. The phased commencement gave employers time to bring older buildings up to standard.

significantAmendments.instrument: HSE Approved Code of Practice L24 (4th edition, 2013)
significantAmendments.effect: Updated guidance with revised minimum standards for temperature, space, sanitary facilities, and rest areas. The ACOP has special legal status — it is not itself law, but if an employer is prosecuted for breach of the Regulations and has not followed the ACOP, a court will find the employer at fault unless the employer can show compliance by other means.

precedingDocuments.title: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (parent Act)
precedingDocuments.significance: The parent Act under which the Workplace Regulations are made. HSWA 1974 s.2 imposes the general duty on employers to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety, and welfare at work of all employees. The Workplace Regulations give specific, enforceable content to this general duty for the physical workplace environment.

precedingDocuments.title: EU Workplace Directive 89/654/EEC
precedingDocuments.significance: The parent EU Directive setting minimum requirements for the workplace. The UK Regulations implemented this Directive. Post-Brexit, the Directive no longer applies but the Regulations remain in force as retained EU law.

precedingDocuments.title: Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963 (repealed)
precedingDocuments.significance: The previous legislation governing workplace conditions in offices and shops. The Workplace Regulations 1992 replaced and consolidated the OSRPA 1963 and the Factories Act 1961 workplace requirements into a single, modern framework.

leadingCases.name: Limited higher court case law — enforcement primarily through HSE prosecution and improvement/prohibition notices
leadingCases.principle: The Workplace Regulations are enforced primarily through HSE inspections, improvement notices, prohibition notices, and prosecution in the magistrates' court or Crown Court. Breach is a CRIMINAL offence under HSWA 1974 s.33, punishable by an unlimited fine. Civil claims for personal injury arising from breach of the Regulations are brought in the county court or High Court. The Sentencing Council Health and Safety Offences Guidelines (2016) apply to determine fine levels based on culpability and harm.
leadingCases.significance: The Workplace Regulations have produced relatively little higher court case law because most breaches are addressed through HSE enforcement action or settled before trial. The practical enforcement risk is through improvement notices (requiring remediation within a specified period) and prohibition notices (requiring immediate cessation of an activity).

parts.title: Part 1 — Scope, Application, and the Employer's Duty

sections.title: Who the Regulations apply to, what constitutes a 'workplace', and the hierarchy of duty holders
sections.summary: The Regulations apply to virtually every workplace in Great Britain. A 'workplace' is defined broadly: any premises or part of premises (not being domestic premises) made available to any person as a place of work. This includes offices, factories, warehouses, shops, hospitals, schools, hotels, restaurants, construction site offices (but NOT the construction site itself — separate CDM Regulations apply), farms, and temporary workplaces. The duty falls primarily on the EMPLOYER for workplaces under their control. Where the workplace is controlled by a person other than the employer (e.g., a landlord of multi-tenant premises), the person in control has the duty to ensure compliance with the Regulations for those parts under their control.
sections.text:
SCOPE — WHAT IS A 'WORKPLACE':
(1) Any premises or part of premises, not being domestic premises, made available to any person as a place of work.
(2) Includes: offices, factories, shops, warehouses, hospitals, schools, hotels, restaurants, laboratories, workshops, garages, depots, farm buildings, leisure centres, museums — ANY premises used for work.
(3) EXCLUDES: construction sites (CDM Regulations 2015), ships (Merchant Shipping regulations), mines below ground (Mines Regulations), domestic premises where the worker is a domestic servant.

DUTY HOLDERS:
(1) EMPLOYER: has the primary duty for workplaces under their control where their employees work.
(2) PERSON IN CONTROL OF PREMISES: where a workplace is not controlled by the employer (e.g., shared/multi-tenant buildings), the person in control (landlord, managing agent) has the duty for shared areas, communal facilities, and building fabric.
(3) SELF-EMPLOYED: owe duties in respect of workplaces they control.

REG.4 — MAINTENANCE:
The workplace, equipment, devices, and systems must be maintained in an efficient state, in efficient working order, and in good repair. Where the maintenance involves a risk to health and safety, appropriate maintenance procedures must be in place.

This is an ABSOLUTE duty — not qualified by 'so far as is reasonably practicable'. The employer must maintain the workplace. If a floor tile is cracked, a handrail is loose, a window is broken, a ventilation system has failed, or a toilet is not functioning — the employer must repair it. There is no defence of cost or inconvenience.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Regulations are UNIVERSAL — they apply to virtually every workplace. The maintenance duty (Reg.4) is ABSOLUTE. The HR manager must understand that these are not aspirational standards — they are legally enforceable minimum requirements. Breach is a criminal offence.
refs: HSWA 1974 s.2 (general employer duty)
HSWA 1974 s.33 (offences — breach of Regulations)
CDM Regulations 2015 (construction sites — separate regime)
HSE ACOP L24 (approved code of practice and guidance)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Workplace health, safety and welfare: Approved Code of Practice and Guidance L24 (4th edition)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The primary guidance document for the Regulations. The ACOP has SPECIAL LEGAL STATUS — non-compliance creates a presumption of breach in criminal proceedings.

commonErrors: Assuming the Regulations only apply to factories or industrial premises — they apply to EVERY workplace including offices, shops, and service businesses. A law firm, an accountancy practice, and a restaurant are all within scope

Not understanding the duty of the person in control of premises — in multi-tenant buildings, the landlord has duties for shared areas (corridors, stairs, lifts, shared toilets, car parks). Tenants cannot assume the landlord is compliant
Treating the maintenance duty (Reg.4) as qualified by reasonableness — it is an ABSOLUTE duty. A broken handrail must be repaired. The employer cannot argue that repairs are too expensive
Not including temporary workplaces — pop-up shops, exhibition stands, temporary offices, and mobile workplaces are all within scope

parts.title: Part 2 — The Working Environment: Ventilation, Temperature, Lighting, Space

sections.title: Environmental standards — the minimum conditions every employer must provide
sections.summary: Regulations 5-11 set the minimum environmental standards for the physical workplace. These are the most frequently engaged provisions and the most common source of employee complaints. The standards cover: ventilation (Reg.6 — effective and suitable provision of fresh or purified air), temperature (Reg.7 — reasonable indoor temperature during working hours with a minimum of 16°C for sedentary work and 13°C for physical work), lighting (Reg.8 — suitable and sufficient, with emergency lighting where needed), cleanliness (Reg.9 — kept sufficiently clean, surfaces capable of being cleaned), room dimensions (Reg.10 — sufficient floor area, height, and unoccupied space for the work being done, with a minimum of 11 cubic metres per person), and workstations (Reg.11 — suitable for the person and the work, with appropriate seating).
sections.text:
REG.6 — VENTILATION:
(1) Every enclosed workplace must have effective and suitable provision for ventilation.
(2) Fresh or purified air must be supplied in sufficient quantities.
(3) Mechanical ventilation systems must be regularly maintained.
(4) Warning devices must be fitted where failure of a ventilation system would pose a health risk.
(5) Employers must be aware of Legionella risks in water-based ventilation systems (cross-reference: HSE guidance on Legionnaires' disease).

REG.7 — TEMPERATURE:
(1) During working hours, the temperature in all workplaces inside buildings shall be REASONABLE.
(2) The ACOP L24 provides minimum temperatures:
— 16°C for sedentary work (offices, call centres, administrative work)
— 13°C for work involving severe physical effort (warehouses, loading bays)
(3) There is NO statutory MAXIMUM temperature — but the employer has a duty to ensure the temperature is 'reasonable'. Where temperatures cannot be reduced to a comfortable level, the employer should provide: fans, air conditioning, cool drinking water, rest breaks in cooler areas, and relaxation of dress codes.
(4) A thermometer must be available in every workplace to enable temperatures to be measured.
(5) Work near hot processes: local heating from furnaces, ovens, or other heat sources must not cause discomfort to workers who are not directly involved in the process.

REG.8 — LIGHTING:
(1) Every workplace must have suitable and sufficient lighting.
(2) Natural lighting should be used where practicable.
(3) Emergency lighting must be provided where sudden loss of artificial light would create a danger.
(4) Lighting must be sufficient for the tasks performed — higher lighting levels are needed for detailed work.
(5) Glare and flickering must be avoided.

REG.9 — CLEANLINESS:
(1) Every workplace and its furniture, furnishings, and fittings must be kept sufficiently clean.
(2) Waste materials must not be allowed to accumulate (except in suitable receptacles).
(3) Floors and indoor traffic routes must be cleaned at least once a week.
(4) Walls, ceilings, and surfaces must be capable of being kept clean.

REG.10 — ROOM DIMENSIONS AND SPACE:
(1) Every room in a workplace must have sufficient floor area, height, and unoccupied space for the purposes of health, safety, and welfare.
(2) ACOP minimum: 11 CUBIC METRES per person (this is NOT floor area — it is VOLUME, calculated as floor area × height). Furniture, fittings, and equipment that take up space must be deducted.
(3) Rooms below 3 metres ceiling height may not provide 11 cubic metres per person even with adequate floor area.

REG.11 — WORKSTATIONS AND SEATING:
(1) Every workstation must be suitable for the person and the work.
(2) Where work can be done seated, a suitable seat must be provided.
(3) A footrest must be provided where necessary.
(4) Outdoor workstations must be arranged so that persons can leave them quickly in an emergency and are protected from adverse weather.
sections.keyPrinciple: These are MINIMUM STANDARDS, not aspirational targets. An employer who provides a workplace that is too cold (below 16°C for sedentary work), poorly ventilated, inadequately lit, or overcrowded (less than 11 cubic metres per person) is committing a criminal offence. The HR manager's responsibility is to ensure these standards are MET and MAINTAINED — not merely documented in a policy.
refs: ACOP L24 (4th edition) — minimum temperature standards, space calculations
HSE guidance on Legionnaires' disease (ventilation systems)
Display Screen Equipment Regulations 1992 (workstation assessment for VDU users — separate but overlapping)
CIBSE design data (lighting and temperature standards)
commonErrors: Believing there is a statutory maximum workplace temperature — there is NOT. But the employer must ensure the temperature is 'reasonable'. During heatwaves, the employer should take practical measures (fans, water, rest breaks, flexible dress codes) rather than claiming there is no legal limit

Calculating space per person using floor area alone — the 11 cubic metres minimum is VOLUME (floor area × ceiling height). A room 4m × 4m × 2.5m = 40 cubic metres, which accommodates only 3 persons (40 ÷ 11 = 3.6, rounded down)
Not maintaining a thermometer in the workplace — Reg.7 requires a thermometer to be available. This is a simple, low-cost requirement that is frequently overlooked

Failing to provide emergency lighting — if sudden loss of artificial light would create a danger (e.g., in a windowless office, a stairwell, or a warehouse), emergency lighting is REQUIRED. This is not optional

Not cleaning the workplace regularly enough — the ACOP specifies that floors and indoor traffic routes must be cleaned at least once a week. In high-risk environments (food preparation, healthcare), more frequent cleaning is required

Ignoring outdoor workstations — workers who work outdoors or in partially open structures (loading bays, external offices, security huts) must be protected from adverse weather and able to leave quickly in an emergency

parts.title: Part 3 — Safety Provisions: Floors, Falls, Traffic Routes, Windows, and Doors

sections.title: Physical safety of the workplace — preventing slips, trips, falls, and collisions
sections.summary: Regulations 12-19 address the physical safety of the workplace structure. These provisions are critically important because slips, trips, and falls remain the MOST COMMON cause of workplace injury in the UK. Reg.12 requires floors and traffic routes to be of suitable construction, free from holes and uneven surfaces, and not slippery. Reg.13 requires protection from falling objects and persons falling from a height. Reg.14 requires transparent doors and walls to be made of safe material or protected against breakage. Reg.17 requires traffic routes for pedestrians and vehicles to be organised to prevent collisions — this is particularly important in warehouses, factories, and any premises where vehicles and pedestrians share space.
sections.text:
REG.12 — FLOORS AND TRAFFIC ROUTES:
(1) Floors must be suitable for the purpose, free from holes, slopes, and uneven/slippery surfaces.
(2) Handrails must be provided on staircases (except where this would obstruct traffic flow).
(3) Floors must be kept free of obstructions that may cause slipping, tripping, or falling.
(4) Adequate drainage must be provided where floors are liable to become wet.

REG.13 — FALLS AND FALLING OBJECTS:
(1) So far as is reasonably practicable, measures must prevent persons falling a distance likely to cause injury.
(2) Measures must prevent objects falling and injuring persons.
(3) Storage of materials must be secure — nothing should be stacked in a way that could collapse.
(4) Tanks, pits, and similar structures must be securely covered or fenced.

REG.14 — TRANSPARENT/TRANSLUCENT DOORS AND WALLS:
(1) Must be made of safety material or protected against breakage.
(2) Must be appropriately marked to make them apparent.

REG.17 — TRAFFIC ROUTES:
(1) Traffic routes must be organised so that pedestrians and vehicles can circulate safely.
(2) Sufficient separation between vehicles and pedestrians.
(3) Where vehicles and pedestrians share routes, sufficient width and adequate marking.
(4) Speed limits, one-way systems, mirrors at blind corners — all may be necessary.
(5) Loading bays must have at least one pedestrian exit point.

Slips, trips, and falls account for approximately 30% of all reported workplace injuries. The HSE's 'Slips and Trips' campaign focuses on Reg.12 compliance. Common causes: wet floors without warning signs, trailing cables, damaged carpet or floor tiles, poor lighting in corridors and stairwells, ice and snow on external paths.
sections.keyPrinciple: Slips, trips, and falls are the SINGLE LARGEST category of workplace injury and the most common basis for civil claims and HSE enforcement. The HR manager should ensure: regular floor inspections, immediate repair of defects, wet floor warning signs, cable management, adequate stairwell lighting, external path gritting in winter, and vehicle-pedestrian segregation where both share the premises.
refs: HSE Slips and Trips guidance (www.hse.gov.uk/slips)
Sentencing Council Health and Safety Offences Guidelines (fine levels)
HSWA 1974 s.2 (general duty — safe workplace)
Work at Height Regulations 2005 (where falls from height are the primary risk)
commonErrors: Not treating wet floor risks seriously — every workplace that mops floors, has entrance areas that get wet in rain, or has processes that produce liquid on floors MUST have a wet floor warning system and immediate cleaning protocols

Failing to segregate pedestrians and vehicles — in warehouses, depots, and delivery areas, vehicle-pedestrian collisions are a serious risk. Marked walkways, barriers, speed limits, and one-way systems are essential
Not maintaining handrails on staircases — handrails must be securely fixed, at the correct height, and available on at least one side. Missing or wobbly handrails are a common enforcement target
Ignoring external paths and car parks — the workplace includes external areas used by employees. Ice, snow, potholes, and poor lighting in car parks and paths are within scope

parts.title: Part 4 — Welfare Facilities: Sanitary Conveniences, Washing, Drinking Water, Rest Areas

sections.title: Welfare facilities — the minimum standards for toilets, washing, drinking water, changing, and rest
sections.summary: Regulations 20-25A set minimum standards for WELFARE FACILITIES — the basic human necessities that every employer must provide. These provisions are frequently underappreciated by HR managers but are some of the most important for employee wellbeing and dignity. Reg.20 requires suitable and sufficient sanitary conveniences (toilets) — with minimum numbers based on the workforce size. Reg.21 requires washing facilities with hot and cold (or warm) running water, soap, and towels (or equivalent). Reg.22 requires an adequate supply of wholesome drinking water, readily accessible, with cups. Reg.23 requires accommodation for clothing not worn during working hours and for drying wet clothing. Reg.24 requires facilities for changing clothing where special clothing is required. Reg.25 requires suitable and sufficient rest facilities — with seating, facilities for eating meals (where food would otherwise be contaminated), and suitable arrangements for pregnant women and nursing mothers.
sections.text:
REG.20 — SANITARY CONVENIENCES (TOILETS):
Minimum numbers (ACOP L24 Table 1):
— 1-5 employees: 1 toilet, 1 washbasin
— 6-25 employees: 2 toilets, 2 washbasins
— 26-50 employees: 3 toilets, 3 washbasins
— 51-75 employees: 4 toilets, 4 washbasins
— 76-100 employees: 5 toilets, 5 washbasins
(Additional facilities for each 25 employees above 100)

Where both men and women work: separate facilities must be provided UNLESS the room is intended for use by one person at a time and can be locked from the inside.

Toilets must be: adequately ventilated, adequately lit, kept in a clean and orderly condition, supplied with toilet paper, and reasonably accessible from workstations.

REG.21 — WASHING FACILITIES:
(1) Suitable and sufficient washing facilities must be provided at readily accessible places.
(2) Must include: hot and cold (or warm) running water, soap (or equivalent), towels or other means of drying.
(3) Showers must be provided where the nature of the work requires it (e.g., dirty, physically demanding, or involving hazardous substances).
(4) Separate facilities for men and women unless each facility is for use by one person at a time and can be locked.

REG.22 — DRINKING WATER:
(1) An adequate supply of wholesome drinking water must be provided.
(2) Must be readily accessible at suitable places.
(3) Marked where necessary to distinguish from non-drinking water.
(4) Suitable cups must be provided (unless the supply is from a drinking fountain).

REG.23 — ACCOMMODATION FOR CLOTHING:
(1) Suitable and sufficient accommodation must be provided for personal clothing not worn during working hours.
(2) Suitable and sufficient accommodation must be provided for special clothing worn only at work.
(3) Facilities for drying clothing must be provided where clothing may become wet during work.

REG.25 — REST FACILITIES:
(1) Suitable and sufficient rest facilities must be provided at readily accessible places.
(2) Rest facilities must include suitable arrangements for eating meals where food would otherwise be contaminated.
(3) Suitable facilities must be provided for pregnant women and nursing mothers to rest.
(4) Rest areas must be equipped with seats and tables and provide protection from tobacco smoke.
sections.keyPrinciple: These are DIGNITY provisions. Every employee is entitled to clean toilets, hot water for washing, free drinking water, somewhere to store personal belongings, and somewhere to rest and eat. An employer who fails to provide these basic facilities is not only committing a criminal offence but is demonstrating a fundamental failure to treat employees with dignity. The HR manager should conduct regular welfare facility audits and ensure that cleaning, maintenance, and restocking are part of the routine facility management programme.
refs: ACOP L24 Table 1 (minimum toilet numbers)
ACOP L24 Table 2 (minimum washbasin numbers)
Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 Reg.16 (new and expectant mothers — risk assessment)
Equality Act 2010 (reasonable adjustments for disabled employees — accessible toilets)
HSE guidance on welfare facilities in the workplace
commonErrors: Not providing enough toilets for the workforce size — the ACOP specifies minimum numbers. An employer with 50 employees who provides only 2 toilets is in breach
Not providing separate toilet facilities for men and women in larger workplaces — separate facilities are required unless each room is individual-use and lockable

Not providing rest facilities for pregnant women and nursing mothers — this is a specific requirement under Reg.25(4), supplemented by the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 Reg.16

Not providing free drinking water — bottled water or water coolers satisfy the requirement, but the employer cannot charge for drinking water. Mains-fed drinking water taps are the simplest compliance route

Failing to provide facilities for drying wet clothing — employees who work outdoors or in wet conditions must have somewhere to dry their clothing. A lack of drying facilities leads to discomfort, illness, and resentment

Not providing accessible facilities for disabled employees — the Equality Act 2010 requires reasonable adjustments including accessible toilets. The HR manager should ensure that welfare facilities are accessible

parts.title: Part 5 — Modern Workplace Challenges: Home Working, Post-COVID, and H&S Consultation

sections.title: Home working, hybrid arrangements, post-COVID ventilation, and the employer's consultation duty
sections.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic fundamentally changed the landscape of the Workplace Regulations. Three issues require specific treatment: (1) HOME WORKING — the Workplace Regulations do NOT apply to domestic premises, but the employer's general duty under HSWA 1974 s.2 and the Display Screen Equipment Regulations 1992 DO apply to home workers. The employer must assess home workstations and provide equipment where necessary. (2) POST-COVID WORKSPACE CONSIDERATIONS — Reg.6 (ventilation) has renewed importance in the context of airborne disease transmission; Reg.10 (space) may need recalculation where occupancy patterns have changed; hybrid working creates variable occupancy that affects all environmental calculations. (3) H&S CONSULTATION — the employer has a statutory duty to consult employees on health and safety matters, either through safety representatives (unionised workplaces) or elected representatives of employee safety (non-unionised workplaces). This consultation duty covers the Workplace Regulations directly.
sections.text:
HOME WORKING — WHAT APPLIES AND WHAT DOES NOT:

(1) The Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992 do NOT apply to domestic premises. An employee's home is NOT a 'workplace' within the meaning of the Regulations.
(2) HOWEVER, the employer's GENERAL DUTY under HSWA 1974 s.2 DOES extend to home workers — the employer must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety, and welfare of employees who work from home.
(3) The Display Screen Equipment (DSE) Regulations 1992 DO apply to home workers who use display screens as a significant part of their work. The employer must: assess the home workstation, provide equipment where necessary (e.g., a suitable chair, screen, keyboard), and ensure the employee takes regular breaks.
(4) The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 require the employer to conduct a risk assessment for ALL employees, including home workers.
(5) In practice, the employer should: provide a home working policy, conduct a home workstation self-assessment (with employer review), provide or fund necessary equipment, and ensure the employee knows how to set up their workstation safely.

POST-COVID WORKSPACE CONSIDERATIONS:

(1) VENTILATION (Reg.6):
— The pandemic highlighted the importance of adequate ventilation for preventing airborne disease transmission.
— The HSE updated its ventilation guidance during the pandemic, recommending: regular air changes, CO2 monitoring as a proxy for ventilation adequacy, opening windows and doors where mechanical ventilation is insufficient, and avoiding recirculation of unfiltered air.
— Employers should: assess ventilation in all enclosed workspaces, consider CO2 monitors as a practical tool, maintain ventilation systems regularly (including filter replacement), and review ventilation when occupancy patterns change.

(2) SPACE (Reg.10):
— Where hybrid working has changed occupancy patterns, the 11 cubic metres per person calculation may need to be reassessed.
— Hot-desking arrangements must still comply with the minimum space requirements at PEAK occupancy, not just average occupancy.
— The employer should not increase desk density simply because some employees are working from home on any given day — the Regulations apply to the MAXIMUM number of people who may be in the workspace at any time.

(3) CLEANING (Reg.9):
— Enhanced cleaning protocols introduced during the pandemic should be maintained where they serve ongoing hygiene purposes.
— Shared workstations (hot-desking) require particular attention to cleaning between users.

H&S CONSULTATION DUTY:

(1) In UNIONISED workplaces: the Safety Representatives and Safety Committees Regulations 1977 (SRSCR) require the employer to consult recognised trade union safety representatives on matters affecting the health, safety, and welfare of employees. Safety representatives have the right to: inspect the workplace, investigate accidents and complaints, attend safety committee meetings, and receive paid time off for their functions.

(2) In NON-UNIONISED workplaces: the Health and Safety (Consultation with Employees) Regulations 1996 (HSCER) require the employer to consult either employees directly or elected representatives of employee safety (ROES) on: the introduction of any measure that may substantially affect their health and safety, arrangements for competent H&S assistance, information required to be provided under H&S legislation, planning and organising H&S training, and the health and safety consequences of introducing new technology.

(3) The consultation duty covers ALL aspects of the Workplace Regulations — temperature, ventilation, welfare facilities, traffic routes, floor conditions. Employees have the right to raise concerns and to be consulted on proposed changes.
sections.keyPrinciple: The modern workplace is not a fixed entity. Hybrid working, home working, and post-COVID health considerations have created a dynamic environment where the employer's obligations must adapt. The Workplace Regulations apply to the physical premises; HSWA and the DSE Regulations extend to home workers; and the consultation duty ensures that employees have a voice in how their working environment is managed. The HR manager must hold all three threads simultaneously.
refs: HSWA 1974 s.2 (general duty — extends to home workers)
Display Screen Equipment Regulations 1992 (home workstation assessment)
MHSWR 1999 Reg.3 (risk assessment — including home workers)
SRSCR 1977 (trade union safety representatives — unionised workplaces)
HSCER 1996 (consultation with employees — non-unionised workplaces)
HSE ventilation guidance (post-COVID updates)
commonErrors: Assuming the Workplace Regulations apply to employees' homes — they do NOT. But the employer's general duty (HSWA s.2) and the DSE Regulations DO extend to home workers. The employer must still assess and support the home working environment

Not conducting home workstation assessments — the DSE Regulations require the employer to assess workstations where employees use display screens as a significant part of their work. A self-assessment questionnaire reviewed by the employer is the minimum acceptable approach

Increasing desk density in the office because some employees work from home — the Reg.10 space calculation applies to the MAXIMUM occupancy, not the average. If all employees could come in on the same day, the space must accommodate them all

Stopping enhanced cleaning protocols entirely after the pandemic — shared workstations and high-traffic areas still benefit from regular cleaning. The Reg.9 standard applies regardless of pandemic status

Not consulting employees on changes to their working environment — the employer has a statutory duty to consult on H&S matters. Changes to ventilation, temperature, workspace layout, and cleaning regimes should be discussed with safety representatives or directly with employees

pendingAmendments.status: The HSE updated ventilation guidance during the COVID-19 pandemic. Reg.6 (ventilation) has renewed importance in the context of airborne disease transmission. Monitor for any formal amendments to the ACOP reflecting post-pandemic ventilation standards.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — employers should ensure ventilation systems meet post-COVID standards

pendingAmendments.status: Campaigns for a statutory maximum workplace temperature (e.g., 30°C for indoor work) have been raised periodically but no legislation has been introduced. Monitor for any Government response to extreme heat events.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: LOW — no legislation imminent but employer duty to ensure 'reasonable' temperature remains
